title: Pasta: ping fails inside LXC Container
body:
### Issue Description

I am running Podman inside a LXC unprivileged Container.

ICMP `ping` Command works inside the LXC Container, both as `root` (UID 0 inside the LXC Container), `podman` (UID 1000 inside the LXC Container) and `testuser` (UID/GID 800000 inside the LXC Container).

ICMP `ping` Command from inside `podman`:
- Works if using `Network=slirp4netns`
- Fails if using `Network=pasta` or `Network=podman`

#### Immediate Cause
After Discussion on IRC with @sbrivio-rh, an Attempt was made to see if there was an "Easy Culprit" for this Issue.

Problem seems to be due to the Function Call `setgroups()` in `isolate_user()`.
If I return before that (https://passt.top/passt/tree/isolation.c#n238 Line 238) then `ping` works.

If I return after that `setgroups()` Call/Block in `isolate_user()` (https://passt.top/passt/tree/isolation.c#n245 Line 245) and before the (next) `setgid()` Block, then `ping` will break and I get the WARNING from `icmp.c`:
```
Cannot open "ping" socket.
You might need to: sysctl -w net.ipv4.ping_group_range="0 2147483647"
...echo requests/replies will fail
```

Not sure how to debug a Function that is declared as `external`.

According to a quick Google Search, it seems to be part of GLIBC:

> The `setgroups` function in Linux is defined in the GNU C Library (glibc), specifically within `<grp.h>` and is implemented in `sysdeps/unix/sysv/linux/setgroups.c`. It requires root privileges (`CAP_SETGID`) to set supplementary group IDs for the calling process.

The Header File of `<grp.h>` is not very self-explanatory to me though:
```
/* Set the group set for the current user to GROUPS (N of them).  */
extern int setgroups (size_t __n, const __gid_t *__groups) __THROW;

/* Store at most *NGROUPS members of the group set for USER into
   *GROUPS.  Also include GROUP.  The actual number of groups found is
   returned in *NGROUPS.  Return -1 if the if *NGROUPS is too small.

   This function is not part of POSIX and therefore no official
   cancellation point.  But due to similarity with an POSIX interface
   or due to the implementation it is a cancellation point and
   therefore not marked with __THROW.  */
```

For the sake of Clarity I copy the `isolate_user()` Function here in order to ensure that even if the Version-Controlled File, the Contents remain clear:
```
void isolate_user(uid_t uid, gid_t gid, bool use_userns, const char *userns,
		  enum passt_modes mode)
{
	uint64_t ns_caps = 0;

	// Debug
	info("[INVESTIGATION] Start of isolate_user()");

	// Until here it works fine

	// Debug
	info("[INVESTIGATION] Before setgroups() in isolate_user()");

	// Return early to avoid dropping Privileges
        // If we return here, then ping will work
        info("[INVESTIGATION] Return early");
	return;

	/* First set our UID & GID in the original namespace */
	if (setgroups(0, NULL)) {
		// Debug
		info("[INVESTIGATION] Set our UID & GID in the original Namespace");

		/* If we don't have CAP_SETGID, this will EPERM */
		if (errno != EPERM)
			die_perror("Can't drop supplementary groups");
	}

	// Debug
	info("[INVESTIGATION] After setgroups() in isolate_user()");

	// Return early to avoid dropping Privileges
        // If we return here, it's already too late, and ping is already broken
        info("[INVESTIGATION] Return early");
	return;

	if (setgid(gid) != 0)
		die_perror("Can't set GID to %u", gid);

	// Debug
	info("[INVESTIGATION] After setgid() in isolate_user()");

	if (setuid(uid) != 0)
		die_perror("Can't set UID to %u", uid);

	// Debug
	info("[INVESTIGATION] After setuid() in isolate_user()");

	if (*userns) { /* If given a userns, join it */
		// Debug
		info("[INVESTIGATION] Join User Namespace %s", userns);

		int ufd;

		ufd = open(userns, O_RDONLY | O_CLOEXEC);
		if (ufd < 0)
			die_perror("Couldn't open user namespace %s", userns);

		if (setns(ufd, CLONE_NEWUSER) != 0)
			die_perror("Couldn't enter user namespace %s", userns);

		close(ufd);

	} else if (use_userns) { /* Create and join a new userns */
		// Debug
		info("[INVESTIGATION] Create and join a new User Namespace");

		if (unshare(CLONE_NEWUSER) != 0)
			die_perror("Couldn't create user namespace");
	}

	// Debug
	info("[INVESTIGATION] After if(*userns) / else if(use_userns) Block in isolate_user()");

	// Debug
	info("[INVESTIGATION] Middle of isolate_user()");

	// Return early to avoid dropping Privileges
	return;

	/* Joining a new userns gives us full capabilities; drop the
	 * ones we don't need.  With --netns-only we haven't changed
	 * userns but we can drop more capabilities now than at
	 * isolate_initial()
	 */
	/* Keep CAP_SYS_ADMIN, so we can unshare() further in
	 * isolate_prefork(), pasta also needs it to setns() into the
	 * netns
	 */
	
	// ns_caps |= BIT(CAP_SYS_ADMIN);
	// if (mode == MODE_PASTA) {
	//	/* Keep CAP_NET_ADMIN, so we can configure the if */
	//	ns_caps |= BIT(CAP_NET_ADMIN);
	//	/* Keep CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE, so we can splice
	//	 * outbound connections to low port numbers
	//	 */
	//	ns_caps |= BIT(CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE);
	//	/* Keep CAP_SYS_PTRACE to join the netns of an
	//	 * existing process */
	//	if (*userns || !use_userns)
	//		ns_caps |= BIT(CAP_SYS_PTRACE);
	//}
	
	// drop_caps_ep_except(ns_caps);

	// Debug
	info("[INVESTIGATION] End of isolate_user()");
}
```

#### Test Container
`pingtester.container`:
```
[Unit]
Description=Pingtester Container

[Container]
ContainerName=pingtester

Image=localhost/docker-network-troubleshooting:debian-20260228
Pull=missing

# Security Configuration
NoNewPrivileges=true

# Networking (for alternative Tests)
# Network=podman
# Network=pasta
# Network=slirp4netns

# Networking: Pasta with save Logs + Debug
Network=pasta:-l,/home/podman/containers/quadlets/pingtester/results/pasta.log,--debug

# Stop immediately, don't wait 10 Seconds before resorting to SIGKILL

# Custom Entrypoint
Entrypoint=/bin/sh
PodmanArgs=-it

[Service]
Restart=always
```

Debug Script `run.sh` to test Changes after each Source Code Modification and save Results + Source Code:
```
#!/bin/bash

# Determine toolpath if not set already
relativepath="./" # Define relative path to go from this script to the root level of the tool
if [[ ! -v test_path_root ]]; then scriptpath=$(cd "$( dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" )" && pwd ); test_path_root=$(realpath --canonicalize-missing "${scriptpath}/${relativepath}"); fi

# Define Container Name
container_name="pingtester"

# Reload Configuration just in Case
systemctl --user daemon-reload

# Debug
echo "Check if Container ${container_name} is currently running"

# Check if Container is running
podman ps --format="{{.Names}}" | grep -q "${container_name}"

if [[ $? -eq 0 ]]
then
    # Stop Container
    systemctl --user stop "${container_name}"
fi

# Remove any previous Log File
if [[ -f "logs/pasta.log" ]]
then
    rm -f "logs/pasta.log"
fi

# Enter a Descriptive for this Run
run_name="$1"
if [[ -z "${run_name}" ]]
then
    # Ask User Interactively
    read -p "Enter a Descriptive Name for this Run: " run_name
fi

# Generate Timestamp
run_timestamp=$(date +"%Y%m%d-%H%M%S")

# Get Current Timestamp in UNIX Timestamp Format
run_timestamp_unix=$(date +"%s")

# Runs Root Path
runs_root_path="${test_path_root}/results"

# Target Path for run Logs
run_path="${runs_root_path}/${run_timestamp}_${run_name}"

# Create Folder for this Run
mkdir -p "${run_path}"

# Restart Container
systemctl --user daemon-reload
systemctl --user restart "${container_name}"

# Execute Ping inside the Container
podman exec "${container_name}" ping -n -4 -c4 -W1 google.com

# View Service Journal
journalctl --user -xeu "${container_name}" --no-pager --since "@${run_timestamp_unix}" | tee "${run_path}/journald_container.log"

# View System Journal
journalctl -xb --no-pager --since "@${run_timestamp_unix}" | tee "${run_path}/journald_system.log"

# Dump Container Information
podman inspect "${container_name}" >> "${run_path}/podman_inspect.txt"

# Dump Podman Info Information
podman info >> "${run_path}/podman_info.txt"

# Stop Container
systemctl --user stop "${container_name}"

# Move Log File
mv "${runs_root_path}/pasta.log" "${run_path}/pasta.log"

# Copy also passt source Code just to be sure we track all parameters
cp -ar "/usr/src/passt" "${run_path}/passt"

# Copy also the Container Script
cp "${test_path_root}/${container_name}.container" "${run_path}/"

# Echo Folder
echo "Run Folder: ${run_path}"
echo "Pasta Run Log: ${run_path}/pasta.log"
```


#### Proxmox VE Host Configuration (Debian like)
`/etc/pve/lxc/203.conf`:
```
arch: amd64
cmode: console
cores: 2
features: fuse=1,keyctl=1,nesting=1
hostname: pingtester
memory: 2048
mp0: /tools_nfs,mp=/tools_nfs,mountoptions=discard;noatime,ro=1
net0: name=eth0,bridge=vmbr0,firewall=1,hwaddr=BC:24:11:FD:CF:AC,type=veth
onboot: 1
ostype: fedora
rootfs: local-zfs:subvol-203-disk-0,mountoptions=discard;noatime,size=64G
swap: 0
tags: template
unprivileged: 1
lxc.idmap: u 0 3000000 1000
lxc.idmap: g 0 3000000 1000
lxc.idmap: u 1000 5003 1
lxc.idmap: g 1000 5003 1
lxc.idmap: u 1001 3001001 900000
lxc.idmap: g 1001 3001001 900000
lxc.cgroup.devices.allow: c 10:200 rwm
lxc.cgroup2.devices.allow: c 10:200 rwm
lxc.mount.entry: /dev/net dev/net none bind,create=dir
```

Notice the UID/GID Map: everything is offsetted by 3000000 (3 Million) and 900000 wide for each different LXC Container (and the next will start at 4 Million and so forth), EXCEPT for the Podman User inside the Container (LXC) `podman` (UID 1000, GID 1000) which is mapped to the Proxmox VE Host User `5003` in this Case for easier identification (and mounting of e.g. NFS Resource from the Host to inside the Container).

`/etc/sysctl.d/99-ping-rootless.conf`:
```
net.ipv4.ping_group_range=0 2147483647
```

Check that it is actually set:
```
root@pve:~# sysctl net.ipv4.ping_group_range
net.ipv4.ping_group_range = 0	2147483647
```

`/etc/subuid`:
```
# Mapped System Users for easier Container Identification
root:5000:1000

# Default Setting for <root>
root:100000:200000

# Allow a lot of subuid for LXC Containers
root:1000000:4294967295
```

`/etc/subgid`:
```
# Mapped System Groups for easier Container Identification
root:5000:1000

# Default Setting for <root>
root:100000:200000

# Allow a lot of subuid for LXC Containers
root:1000000:4294967295
```

#### LXC Container (Fedora)
`/etc/subuid`:
```
root:0:65536
podman:100000:65536
```

`/etc/subgid`:
```
root:0:65536
podman:100000:65536
```

`/etc/sysctl.d/99-ping-rootless.conf`:
```
net.ipv4.ping_group_range=0 2147483647
```

Check that it is actually set:
```
[root@pingtester src]# sysctl net.ipv4.ping_group_range
net.ipv4.ping_group_range = 0	901000
```

I tried several Settings in `/home/podman/.config/containers/containers.conf` to verify that there is not some "Offset" Problem that maybe `podman` was compensating for (which it shouldn't have) or vice-versa if `podman` was not compensating for an offset (which it should have):
```
# A list of sysctls to be set in containers by default,
# specified as "name=value",
# for example:"net.ipv4.ping_group_range=0 0".
#
# default_sysctls = [
#   "net.ipv4.ping_group_range=0 0",
# ]
default_sysctls = [
#  "net.ipv4.ping_group_range=0 2147483647",
#  "net.ipv4.ping_group_range=0 900000",
#  "net.ipv4.ping_group_range=0 165536",
#  "net.ipv4.ping_group_range=0 160000",
  "net.ipv4.ping_group_range=0 65536",
#  "net.ipv4.ping_group_range=100000 165536",
#  "net.ipv4.ping_group_range=100000 160000",
#  "net.ipv4.ping_group_range=0 0",
]
```

Ultimately anything wider and/or with a higher offset than "net.ipv4.ping_group_range=0 65536"` would just throw an Error when attempting to start the Container: `conmon c44884429ed1759d8795 <nwarn>: runtime stderr: write to `/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ping_group_range`: Invalid argument`.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Setup Container according to `pingtester.container` using e.g. my [Image for Troubleshooting](https://github.com/luckylinux/docker-network-troubleshooting) (or any other Image, but you'll need to install the `ping` Command)
2. Run Container
3. Try to `ping -n -4 -c4 -W1 google.com` inside the Container
4. Command will hang. Check the Logs with `systemctl -xb --reverse`. Checking the Service Log only with `systemctl --user -xeu pingtester.service` will NOT show the Pasta Message. You might need to add the Podman User to the `systemd-journal` Group with `gpasswd -a podman systemd-journal` and re-login after that for the Changes to take effect.

### Additional environment details

1. Baremetal Host: Proxmox VE AMD64 (`pve-manager/9.1.6/71482d1833ded40a (running kernel: 6.17.13-1-pve)`)
2. LCX Container: Fedora 43 AMD64 (unprivileged)
3. Podman Container: Debian 13 using my own [Image for Troubleshooting](https://github.com/luckylinux/docker-network-troubleshooting), although I believe also other Containers will behave very similarly. This Image is convenient because it packs a lot of common Troubleshooting Tools.

_No response_

comments.body:
@luckylinux This is top-tier debugging. You've pinpointed exactly where pasta's isolation strategy conflicts with unprivileged LXC containers.

Since setgroups(0, NULL) returns success, pasta thinks it has safely isolated itself, but in doing so, it strips the supplementary groups that LXC injected to allow networking (likely related to the mapped GIDs or socket permissions on the host).

Since you are already talking to @sbrivio-rh, this is definitely an upstream fix in passt (it needs a flag or check to preserve groups in this specific environment). Podman can't really fix this until passt releases a patch.

Thanks for the detailed workaround.

Thank you 😃.

It's not really a workaround because you will need to recompile `pasta` each Time there is a System Update 🤣.

I was also thinking that maybe adding a `--ignore-setgroups` or whatever named flag to skip that Block could be an Option.

Alternatively it could be possible to autodetect if running inside an LXC Container using e.g.:
```
podman@pingtester:~/containers/quadlets/pingtester$ mount -l  | grep lxc
proc on /dev/.lxc/proc type proc (rw,relatime)
sys on /dev/.lxc/sys type sysfs (rw,relatime)
```

The "trick" about looking into `/proc/1/cgroup` doesn't seem to be working anymore.

`cat /proc/1/sched | head -n1` could be used to detect if running inside a Podman Container, although it doesn't seem to be working for an LXC Container (`systemd` is marked as init system anyways):
- Proxmox VE Host: `cat /proc/1/sched | head -n1`  -> `systemd (1, #threads: 1)`
- LXC Container: `cat /proc/1/sched | head -n1`  -> `systemd (1, #threads: 1)`
- Podman Container: `cat /proc/1/sched | head -n1`  -> `sh (1, #threads: 1)` (but I guess it could also be `bash`)

Easiest is probably to just to check if `/dev/.lxc` exists for an LXC Container.

For detecting Podman/Docker Container I'm not quite sure. Maybe `/proc/self/cgroup` 🤔 ?
- Proxmox VE Host: `0::/user.slice/user-0.slice/session-94.scope`
- LXC Container: `cat /proc/self/cgroup` -> `0::/user.slice/user-1000.slice/session-c8.scope`
- Podman Container: `cat /proc/self/cgroup` -> `0::/`

The Question is however: is disabling `setgroups` the correct Approach to take ?

Networking works anyways, it's "just" `ping` that was broken.

> Since setgroups(0, NULL) returns success, pasta thinks it has safely isolated itself, but in doing so, it strips the supplementary groups that LXC injected to allow networking (likely related to the mapped GIDs or socket permissions on the host).

Why does LXC need extra groups? The sysctl should still allow anybody to ping basically so why is there an extra restriction?

AFAIK from past conversation @sbrivio-rh was always against any option that disables/weakens the sandboxing.

As discussed with @sbrivio-rh on IRC Channel, it seems that the Issue stems from dropping the Group `nobody` (GID 65534) which is the default unprivileged User on Proxmox VE:

https://pve.proxmox.com/wiki/Unprivileged_LXC_containers

My Understanding is that, since `root` starts the LXC Container on the Proxmox VE Host, then `/proc` inside the LXC Container will be owned by:
- `nobody` (65534) for unprivileged Containers
- `root` (0) for privileged Containers

Furthermore any UID/GID Mapping that we want to do from Proxmox VE Host to LXC Container needs to be allowed for User `root`, e.g.:

`/etc/subuid`:
```
# Mapped System Users for easier Container Identification
root:5000:1000

# Default Setting for <root>
root:100000:200000

# Allow a lot of subuid for LXC Containers
root:1000000:4294967295
```

`/etc/subgid`:
```
# Mapped System Groups for easier Container Identification
root:5000:1000

# Default Setting for <root>
root:100000:200000

# Allow a lot of subuid for LXC Containers
root:1000000:4294967295
```


The Quick Fix is to make sure that User 65534 (`nobody`) is NOT dropped.

To re-iterate what I said Yesterday: we were NOT getting errors writing to `procfs` because the function `pasta_start_ns` was never called to begin with.
In `conf.c` Function `void conf(struct ctx *c, int argc, char **argv)`, `*netns` is set and therefore `pasta_open_ns(c, netns);` is called.
Since `pasta_start_ns(c, uid, gid, argc - optind, argv + optind);` wasn't getting called, there were no attempts to write to procfs anyways.

So in my view it it for sure a `procfs` write Issue, just one that we didn't see with the "standard" pasta, since due to that `if(*userns)` statement, `pasta_open_ns(c, netns);` is called instead.


There probably is also something else to it, but I wouldn't jump to to the conclusion that it isn't a procfs write issuee, since it was never tested ;)
